learning_outcomes.description: Understand and apply a culturally safe practice.
Describe Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander and global Indigenous health in a historical context and analyse the impact of colonial processes on First Nation health outcomes.
Analyse, apply and critically assess the role of the determinants of health to global and national First Nations health outcomes.
Apply a health equity framework to critically analyse the strengths of First Nations led health initiatives.

Critically evaluate and apply participatory, collaborative, and advocacy approaches to develop sustainable, culturally safe, equitable and responsive health policies, services, and systems that support First Nations health and wellbeing.
Demonstrate skills in written and oral communication, advocacy, reflection, digital capabilities, and teamwork.

assessments.assessment_title: Critical Reflexivity Portfolio Through Decolonising Lens
assessments.due_date:
VLog 1 (Cultural Standpoint and Culturally Safe Practice) 20/08/2026 2:00 pm

VLog 2 Advocacy and Health Systems) 17/09/2026 2:00 pm

Students must complete ALL assessments to pass this course.
assessments.task_description:
A Critical Reflexive Portfolio is a digital collection of your reflexive inquiries into big questions and themes explored throughout the semester in INDH7002. These reflections are presented in the form of Video Logs (VLogs). Developing a strong reflexive practice is foundational o becoming a respectful, culturally sensitive, equitable, and well-informed practitioner, scholar, and ally.

Reflexivity is vital to understanding how cultural paradigms and our own experiences influence our worldviews, unconscious biases, and philosophy in healthcare and research.

From Weeks 1- 8, you are required to complete self-directed weekly learning modules in Blackboard Learn Ultra. Each module contains guided activities and reflective questions designed to help you:

  • critically engage with the learning materials;
  • examine your personal assumptions, values, and blind spots;
  • reflect on your positionality and cultural standpoint; and
  • consider the implications of your learning for culturally safe practice.


The weekly activities, including self-directed modules, lectures, Deadly Yarn Podcasts and scholarly literature, will form the foundation of your Critical Reflexive Portfolio. This is intended to be a personal learning space in which you engage with First Nations health and wellbeing content, critically reflect on you learning process through a reflexive and decolonising lens, and deepen your understanding of culturally safe practice. You are expected to integrate scholarly evidence into your reflexivity.

 Your Critical Reflexive Portfolio consists of two VLog submissions: 

  1. VLog 1: Cultural Standpoint and Culturally Safe Practice. This VLog draws on learnings from Weeks 1 - 4. Due Week 4 (15%)
  2. VLog 2: Advocacy and Health Systems. This VLog draws on learnings from Weeks 5 - 8. Due Week (15%).


Learning objectives assessed: 1, 2, 3, 6

Please refer to Blackboard Learn Ultrafor details on Assessment 1.
assessments.submission_guidelines: All assessment items are conducted via the ULTRA Blackboard site. This includes all Undergraduate and Postgraduate (internal and external) courses. Instructions are provided for each assessment item on each course site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Critical Analysis of a Contemporary Indigenous Health Equity Issue
assessments.due_date:
8/10/2026

Students must complete ALL assessments to pass this course.
assessments.task_description:
Understanding and improving health equity requires health professionals to critically examine the historical, social, cultural and structural factors that influence the health experiences of First Nations peoples.

In this assessment, you will critically analyse a contemporary health equity challenge affecting First Nations peoples in Australia or globally. Drawing on concepts explored throughout INDH7002, you will examine how social, cultural, historical, and structural determinants of health shape health outcomes and contribute to ongoing inequities for First Nations peoples.

You will apply evidence from academic literature, government reports and action plans such as Closing the Gap targets (https://www.closingthegap.gov.au/national-agreement), and the First Nations scholarship to identify and critically discuss the determinants influencing your chosen equity issue. The focus of this assessment is on developing an evidence-based understanding of the issue and the factors that contribute to health inequities for First Nations peoples.

This assessment builds your capacity to work respectfully and effectively within Indigenous health contexts and prepares you for Assessment 3, where you will develop culturally safe, equity-focused recommendations and advocacy strategies.

Learning objectives assessed: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

Please refer to Blackboard Learn Ultra for details on Assessment 2.
assessments.submission_guidelines: All assessment items are conducted via the ULTRA Blackboard site. This includes all Undergraduate and Postgraduate (internal and external) courses. Instructions are provided for each assessment item on each course site.

assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
As a public health consultant, you have been invited to work with a mainstream (non-Indigenous-led) organisation. The organisation has recently become aware of the ‘Making Tracks Together’: Queensland's Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Health Equity Framework and has requested a meeting with its leadership team. You have been asked to present the potential value, opportunities, and organisational benefits of implementing the Health Equity Framework.

Your task is to deliver an oral presentation that pitches the relevance and value of the Health Equity Framework to leaders of an organisation committed to advancing health equity for First Nations Peoples.

This assessment builds on the critical analysis of a health equity issue undertaken in Assessment 2. You are required to creatively and critically communicate the significance of the Health Equity Framework and demonstrate how its implementation can support culturally safe, respectful, and effective engagement with First Nations communities. In doing so, you should consider how the Framework contributes to addressing racial health inequities and strengthening organisational accountability within mainstream services working with First Nations Peoples.

Learning objectives assessed: 1, 4, 5, 6

Please refer to Blackboard Learn Ultra for details on Assessment 1.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1: Introduction to First Nations Health and Wellbeing

This session will introduce INDH7002, outlining what will be covered over the 13 weeks, including key topic areas covered in the course, assessments, and expectations. Students will gain an overview of the course structure, learning outcomes, and how each assessment supports their understanding of First Nations health and wellbeing in both Australian and global contexts.


Sub-activity: Pre-tutorial: Deadly Yarn Podcast: Introduction to decolonising global health Tutorial 1: Introduction to course expectations, assessments, AI & secured assessment and introduction to self-directed leanring module

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2: Identity & Culture: Indigenous Ways of Knowing, Being and Doing

This session explores the foundational concepts of Indigenous identity and culture, focusing on First Nations ways of knowing, being, and doing. Students will be introduced to Indigenous epistemologies and ontologies, which shape how knowledge is understood, lived, and shared within communities.


Sub-activity: Self-directed weekly module on cultural and personal standpoint. Tutorial 2: What does it mean to learn on Country (and on stolen lands)?
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 2: Identity & Culture: Indigenous Ways of Knowing, Being and Doing
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2: Identity & Culture: Indigenous Ways of Knowing, Being and Doing This session explores the foundational concepts of Indigenous identity and culture, focusing on First Nations ways of knowing, being, and doing. Students will be introduced to Indigenous epistemologies and ontologies, which shape how knowledge is understood, lived, and shared within communities. Sub-activity: Self-directed weekly module on cultural and personal standpoint. Tutorial 2: What does it mean to learn on Country (and on stolen lands)?

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3: Hidden Histories: First Nations Perspectives on Resistance and Resilience in Pre- and Post- Colonial Context

This session uncovers the often-overlooked histories of First Nations resistance and resilience across both pre- and post-colonial periods. Students will engage with Indigenous perspectives that challenge dominant historical narratives, highlighting the strength, agency, and survival of First Nations Peoples in the face of colonisation, dispossession, and systemic injustice. Through storytelling, historical accounts, and critical analysis, the session will explore how resistance has taken many forms—from cultural continuity and land protection to political activism and community leadership. It will also examine the enduring resilience of First Nations communities, grounded in deep cultural knowledge, kinship systems, and spiritual connection to Country. Students will be encouraged to reflect on the importance of truth-telling, historical justice, and the role of education in supporting reconciliation and equity.


Sub-activity: Pre-tutorial: Listen to Deadly Yarn Podcast: Hidden Histories: Resistance and resilience of colonisation of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples Tutorial 3: Self-directed culturally safe practice Part 1

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4: Global Indigenous Peoples and Perspectives in Indigenous Health

This session explores the diverse experiences and perspectives of Global Indigenous Peoples in relation to health and wellbeing. Students will examine how colonisation, systemic inequities, and cultural resilience shape Indigenous health outcomes across different global contexts, including Aotearoa (New Zealand), Canada, the United States, and the Pacific. The session will highlight shared challenges—such as dispossession, marginalisation, and health disparities—as well as the strength of Indigenous knowledge systems, community-led health initiatives, and cultural revitalisation efforts. Students will be encouraged to reflect on the importance of global solidarity, Indigenous sovereignty, and culturally grounded approaches to health that honour Indigenous ways of knowing, being, and doing.


Sub-activity: Tutorial 4: Reflections on panel session and using AI as a culturally safe learning tool in Indigenous context

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5: Understanding Determinants in Indigenous Health

This session explores the social, cultural, historical, and political determinants that shape the health and wellbeing of First Nations Peoples. Students will critically examine how colonisation, systemic racism, intergenerational trauma, and socio-economic disadvantage contribute to ongoing health inequities. Students will be encouraged to reflect on how these determinants intersect and how health professionals can respond in ways that are culturally safe, respectful, and equity-focused.


Sub-activity: Pre-tutorial Deadly Yarn Podcast: Social and cultural determinants of health: cultural identity, connection to land, culture and language and self-determination! Tutorial 5: Introducing Advocacy and Health Systems Modules

Students will critically examine how colonisation, systemic racism, intergenerational trauma, and socio-economic disadvantage contribute to ongoing health inequities. Students will be encouraged to reflect on how these determinants intersect and how health professionals can respond in ways that are culturally safe, respectful, and equity-focused. Sub-activity: Pre-tutorial Deadly Yarn Podcast: Social and cultural determinants of health: cultural identity, connection to land, culture and language and self-determination! Tutorial 5: Introducing Advocacy and Health Systems Modules

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6: Rights-Based Approach to Health & Health Equity

This session introduces students to a rights-based approach to health, emphasising the importance of human rights, social justice, and equity in addressing health disparities experienced by First Nations Peoples. Students will explore how international and national frameworks—such as the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP)—inform ethical and culturally safe health practices. The session will critically examine how systemic barriers and discriminatory policies have impacted Indigenous health outcomes, and how a rights-based lens can support advocacy, accountability, and community empowerment. Students will also consider the role of health professionals and institutions in upholding the rights of Indigenous Peoples through inclusive, respectful, and equity-driven approaches. Through case studies, students will reflect on how rights-based principles intersect with Indigenous worldviews and contribute to meaningful change in health systems.

Sub-activity: Pre-tutorial: Listen to Deadly Yarn Podcast: Racism as a Structural Determinant of Health Tutorial 6: Self-directed Advocacy and Health Systems Part 1

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7: Racism as a Structural Determinant of Health

This session critically examines racism as a structural determinant of health, with a focus on its impact on First Nations Peoples in Australia and Indigenous communities globally. Students will explore how racism operates across systems—including healthcare, education, housing, and justice—to produce and sustain health inequities.

The session will unpack the difference between interpersonal, institutional, and systemic racism and how these intersect with other social determinants. Students will also engage with frameworks that support anti-racist and decolonising approaches in public health practice.

By the end of the session, students will be able to identify how racism shapes health outcomes, reflect on their own positionality, and consider strategies for promoting equity and accountability within health systems.

Sub-activity: Podcast - Racism and Anti-racism! Tutorial 7: Navigating health systems – Case of Rheumatic Heart Disease (RHD) in Doomadgee

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8: Health Systems & Policy: Political Determinants of Health

This session examines the political determinants of health and their influence on health systems, policies, and outcomes for First Nations Peoples. Students will explore how political ideologies, governance structures, legislation, and resource allocation shape access to healthcare and contribute to systemic inequities. Through a decolonising lens, the session will critically analyse the role of policy-making in perpetuating or challenging health disparities, and highlight the importance of Indigenous leadership, advocacy, and sovereignty in shaping health systems that are culturally safe and responsive. Students will engage with case studies and policy frameworks to understand how political decisions impact health equity and reflect on the responsibilities of health professionals in promoting justice and accountability within public health systems.

Sub-activity: Tutorial 8: Introduction to Assessment 2: Critical Analysis of HealtH Equity Challenge Isuue Activity: Racism in Healthcare and Access to Culturally Safe Care. How institutional and interpersonal racism affects access to healthcare and health outcomes of Doomadgee community. Students can analyse hpw power structures, health systems, cultural safety, and policy responses to racism.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9: Health Systems: Indigenous Community Controlled Health Services in Public Health Practice

This session explores the role of Aboriginal Community Controlled Health Organisation Services (ACCHOS) and the National Aboriginal Community Controlled Health Organisation(NACHOS: https://www.naccho.org.au/) within the broader Australian health system, emphasising their significance in advancing public health outcomes for First Nations peoples. Students will learn from First Nations voices and scholarship, gaining insight into the principles of self-determination, holistic care, and community empowerment that underpin NACHOS and ACCHOS. By the end of the session, students will be equipped to apply culturally responsive strategies in public health practice and advocate for systems-level change that supports Indigenous-led health initiatives.

 

Sub-activity: Pre-Tutorial: Listen to Deadly Yarn Podcast: Evidence strength-based approach: Community-led programmes to improve Indigenous Health Tutorial 9: Introduction to Assessment 3, preparing for heath equity pitch: Task expectations & marking criteria .

Students will learn from First Nations voices and scholarship, gaining insight into the principles of self-determination, holistic care, and community empowerment that underpin NACHOS and ACCHOS. learning_activities.topic:
Week 10: Community Led: Co-design & Leadership

This session explores the principles and practices of co-design and leadership in the development and implementation of community-based health programmes, with a particular focus on Indigenous communities. Grounded in a strengths-based approach, the session highlights how Indigenous leadership, cultural knowledge, and community priorities shape effective and sustainable health interventions. Students will examine real-world examples of community-led initiatives that have improved health outcomes by fostering empowerment, resilience, and self-determination. The session emphasises collaborative methods that value Indigenous voices throughout the design, delivery, and evaluation of health services. Through critical reflection and case-based learning, students will develop skills in ethical engagement, culturally safe practice, and systems thinking to support Indigenous-led health innovation and leadership.

Sub-activity: Tutorial 10: Preparing for your health equity pitch: co-design and working with First Nations communities.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11: Community Advocacy: Implications for Policy and Practice

This session examines community advocacy in shaping health policy and practice with a particular focus on Indigenous health equity. Students will explore how grassroots movements, community-led initiatives, and Indigenous leadership influence systemic change and challenge dominant policy frameworks. The session highlights the mechanisms by which advocacy translates into policy reform, improved service delivery, and culturally responsive health systems. Emphasis is placed on the role of public health professionals in supporting advocacy efforts ethically and effectively, while amplifying Indigenous voices and priorities. Students will gain practical insights into policy engagement, coalition-building, and strategic communication, equipping them to contribute meaningfully to advocacy efforts that promote justice, equity, and self-determination in health.

Sub-activity:

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12: Reconciliation and Working as an Ally in Solidarity for First Nations Health Equity

This session focuses on the role of non-Indigenous allies in advancing health equity for First Nations peoples through respectful, informed, and sustained solidarity. It explores the principles of reconciliation, allyship, and ethical engagement, emphasising how allies can support Indigenous-led health initiatives. Students will critically examine the historical and systemic factors contributing to health disparities and learn how to engage in culturally safe practices that centre Indigenous voices, leadership, and self-determination. The session highlights the importance of humility, accountability, and ongoing learning in allyship and provides practical strategies for working collaboratively within health systems to promote equity and justice.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 13: Reflection on Your Learning Journey: Upbeat Q & A Discussion & Course Evaluation

This final session offers a dynamic and engaging space for students to reflect on their personal and collective learning journey throughout the course. The session encourages open dialogue, storytelling, and shared insights, fostering a sense of closure and celebration. Students will have the opportunity to ask questions, share reflections, and explore how their learning can be applied in future public health practice. Emphasis will be placed on growth, transformation, and continuing the journey of respectful engagement and advocacy for First Nations health equity. Additionally, students will be invited to share their feedback through the course evaluation (SECaT), contributing to the continuous improvement of the learning experience. At the end of this course, we hope students feel inspired, connected, and confident in their role as culturally responsive public health practitioners
